skill.name: azure-eventgrid-java
skill.description: Build event-driven applications with Azure Event Grid SDK for Java. Use when publishing events, implementing pub/sub patterns, or integrating with Azure services via events.
skill.summary: Build event-driven applications with Azure Event Grid SDK for Java. Use when publishing events, impl...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 48 static findings are false positives. The scanner detected markdown code fences and inline code markers (backticks) as shell commands, placeholder URLs as hardcoded endpoints, JSON placeholder syntax as path traversal, and benign documentation text as cryptographic/reconnaissance patterns. This is a legitimate Azure Event Grid Java SDK documentation file with no actual security risks.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner detected backticks in markdown as shell command execution. These are code fences (```java, ```xml) and inline code markers, not actual commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Markdown documentation file uses backticks only for code formatting. No shell or Ruby execution present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URL (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner flagged placeholder URL format. This is example documentation format, not a real endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: URL uses placeholder syntax <topic-name> and <region>, standard documentation practice.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Traversal (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner detected ../ in JSON placeholder. This is JSON syntax for placeholder ellipsis, not file path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line contains JSON example with '...' placeholder, not actual path traversal code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner misidentified documentation text as cryptographic code. No cryptographic algorithms present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Lines contain metadata, table headers, and generic text. No cryptographic code exists in this documentation file.

dangerous_patterns.title: System Reconnaissance (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner flagged Azure service names as reconnaissance. These are legitimate Azure SDK references.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: References to Azure services (Event Grid, Storage, CloudEvents) are legitimate SDK documentation, not reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Build Event-Driven Apps with Azure Event Grid Java SDK
content.value_statement: Integrate Azure Event Grid into Java applications to publish and subscribe to events using a reliable cloud-native pub/sub messaging system.
actual_capabilities: Create EventGridPublisherClient with API key or Azure Identity credentials
Publish single or batch events using EventGridEvent or CloudEvent schemas
Parse and handle incoming events from webhooks
Receive events from Event Grid Namespaces with MQTT or pull delivery
Handle system events from Azure services like Storage and Event Hubs
Implement async event publishing for better performance
limitations: Requires Azure subscription and Event Grid topic setup
Event size limit of 1MB for standard tier, 64KB for basic tier
Does not include webhook server implementation - only client SDK
Requires network access to Azure Event Grid endpoints

use_cases.title: Java Developer Building Cloud Applications
use_cases.description: Integrate event-driven architecture into Java microservices to decouple components and enable scalable asynchronous communication.
use_cases.target_user: Java backend developers working with Azure cloud services

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Automating Workflows
use_cases.description: Set up event triggers from Azure resources to automate CI/CD pipelines, monitoring alerts, and operational workflows.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers automating Azure resource event handling

use_cases.title: Solutions Architect Designing Event Systems
use_cases.description: Design pub/sub patterns using CNCF CloudEvents specification for vendor-neutral event streaming across hybrid cloud environments.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Event Publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an EventGridPublisherClient and publish a single event using the Azure Event Grid Java SDK.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with basic event publishing

prompt_templates.title: Batch Event Publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I publish multiple events in a single batch call using the Azure Event Grid SDK for Java?

prompt_templates.title: CloudEvents Format
prompt_templates.prompt: Demonstrate how to publish events using the CloudEvents 1.0 schema format with the Azure Event Grid Java library.

prompt_templates.title: Event Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to receive and parse incoming EventGridEvent and CloudEvent payloads in Java, including handling system events.

output_examples.input: Create an Event Grid publisher client using Azure Identity for authentication
output_examples.output: Use DefaultAzureCredentialBuilder to create a client that authenticates via managed identity, service principal, or developer credentials without embedding secrets in code.

output_examples.input: Publish a custom event with JSON data to Event Grid topic
output_examples.output: Create EventGridEvent with subject, eventType, BinaryData payload, and dataVersion, then send via client.sendEvent().

output_examples.input: Handle Azure Storage blob creation events
output_examples.output: Subscribe to Microsoft.Storage.BlobCreated event type and parse StorageBlobCreatedEventData to get the blob URL and properties.

best_practices: Use batch publishing (sendEvents) instead of single events to reduce HTTP overhead and improve throughput
Include unique event IDs for idempotent event handling and duplicate detection
Use DefaultAzureCredential or managed identities instead of API keys for production workloads
anti_patterns: Embedding access keys directly in source code instead of using Azure Key Vault or managed identities
Sending events synchronously in high-throughput scenarios without async client or batching
Ignoring dead-letter configuration for events that fail delivery after retry attempts

faq.question: What is the difference between EventGridEvent and CloudEvent?
faq.answer: EventGridEvent is Azure's native schema, while CloudEvent follows the CNCF CloudEvents 1.0 specification for vendor-neutral event formatting.

faq.question: How do I authenticate to Event Grid without API keys?
faq.answer: Use DefaultAzureCredentialBuilder which supports managed identity, service principal, Visual Studio credentials, and Azure CLI authentication.

faq.question: What is the maximum event size I can send?
faq.answer: Standard tier allows up to 1MB per event. Basic tier is limited to 64KB per event.

faq.question: Can I use this SDK to receive events from a webhook?
faq.answer: Yes, use EventGridEvent.fromString() or CloudEvent.fromString() to parse incoming JSON payloads from webhooks.

faq.question: What are Event Grid Namespaces?
faq.answer: Namespaces provide MQTT broker and pull delivery for modern event-driven architectures, offering more control over event consumption.

faq.question: How do I handle failed event deliveries?
faq.answer: Configure dead-letter queues for events that fail delivery after retry attempts. Use reject() and release() methods for manual handling.
